flashcards.front: Define locality of reference and its two types in memory hierarchy.
flashcards.back: Locality of reference is the tendency of a processor to access the same memory locations repeatedly. Temporal locality means recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again, while spatial locality means items near recently accessed locations are likely to be accessed soon. This principle underlies the effectiveness of cache memory.

flashcards.front: How is average memory access time (AMAT) calculated for a cache system?
flashcards.back: AMAT = Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty). For a two-level cache system, AMAT = L1 hit time + (L1 miss rate × L2 hit time) + (L1 miss rate × L2 miss rate × Main memory access time). This metric helps evaluate cache performance.

flashcards.front: Compare write-through and write-back cache write policies.
flashcards.back: Write-through writes data to both cache and main memory on every write operation, ensuring data consistency but increasing memory traffic. Write-back writes data only to cache initially and marks the block as dirty; it writes to main memory only when the block is replaced, reducing memory writes but requiring complex hardware for coherence.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between direct mapping, fully associative mapping, and set-associative mapping for cache memory.
flashcards.back: In direct mapping, each memory block maps to exactly one cache line using a modulo operation. In fully associative mapping, any block can occupy any line. Set-associative mapping divides cache into sets where each block maps to a specific set but can occupy any line within it (e.g., 4-way). Direct mapping is simple but prone to conflict misses; fully associative has best hit rate but expensive hardware; set-associative balances performance and cost.

flashcards.front: What is virtual memory and how does it extend the addressable memory space?
flashcards.back: Virtual memory uses disk space to extend apparent RAM size by mapping virtual addresses to physical addresses via a page table. When a requested page is not in RAM (page fault), the OS brings it from disk, allowing programs larger than physical RAM to run while providing memory protection and process isolation.

flashcards.front: Define seek time, rotational latency, and transfer time for magnetic disk storage.
flashcards.back: Seek time is the time to position the read/write head over the correct track (5-15 ms). Rotational latency is the wait for the desired sector to rotate under the head (half rotation, 4-8 ms at 7200 RPM). Transfer time is the actual data read/write time (depends on size and rate). Total access time ≈ Seek time + Rotational latency + Transfer time.

flashcards.front: Compare SRAM, DRAM, and Flash memory in terms of volatility, speed, cost, and typical use in memory hierarchy.
flashcards.back: SRAM is volatile, fastest, most expensive, used for CPU cache (L1/L2/L3). DRAM is volatile, slower but cheaper than SRAM, used for main memory. Flash is non-volatile, slower than both but cheapest and durable, used for secondary storage (SSDs, USB drives) in auxiliary memory. Hierarchy: Registers → SRAM Cache → DRAM Main Memory → Flash/Disk Storage.

flashcards.front: A computer system has a cache with hit time of 1 cycle, miss penalty of 100 cycles, and hit ratio of 0.95. Calculate the average memory access time (AMAT).
flashcards.back: AMAT = Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty) = 1 + ((1-0.95) × 100) = 1 + (0.05 × 100) = 1 + 5 = 6 cycles. This shows that even with a 95% hit ratio, the miss penalty significantly impacts performance, emphasizing the importance of reducing miss rate and miss penalty.
